perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) des ignated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person should recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the c ircumstances of its disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person *may* recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the circumstances of its disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "should" to "may" introduces uncertainty about what constitutes Confidential Information. Originally, the Receiving Party was obligated to treat information a reasonable person *should* recognize as confidential as such. Now, with "may," it's less clear, creating a contradiction. This uncertainty is relevant because it undermines the purpose of Section 2, which clearly defines Confidential Information for the purposes of this agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person may recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the circumstances of its disclosure. By way of example, Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information regarding a Party’s products, product development, services, agreements and contracts, business operations, business plans and financial affairs; employees and other personnel; vendors and customers; marketing plans and strategies; processes, compensation formulas, methods and specifications; and information technology network. Additionally, t he fact of the Parties discussions regarding a p otential business relationship shall be deemed Confidential Information subject to the protections of this Agreement. Confidential Information may be either the property of the Disclosing Party or information provided to the Disclosing Party by a third party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "should" to "may" in the definition of Confidential Information introduces a significant ambiguity. The original text used "should," establishing a clearer objective standard for what constitutes confidential information. The changed text uses "may," which makes the recognition of confidential information subjective and discretionary. This directly contradicts the purpose of defining "Confidential Information" in the first place. The Contradicted Text explicitly uses that definition, and now that definition is ambiguous, making it unclear what falls under Confidential Information, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party *may*, upon the written request of the Disclosing Party, return to the Disclos ing Party or, at the Disclosing Party’s direction, destroy all documents and any and all other tangible manifestations of Confidential Information received or created by the Receiving Party pursuant to this Agreement (and all copies and reproductions there of).
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "may" weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request. This directly contradicts the implied duty to protect Confidential Information outlined in Section 4. Section 4 states the Receiving Party 'shall hold all Confidential Information in confidence'. Thus, making the return of confidential information optional weakens the obligation to hold all Confidential Information in confidence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Disclosure and Protection of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose and for no other purpose without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall" to "may" in the Changed Text significantly weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request. This directly conflicts with the Contradicted Text, which states the Receiving Party "shall hold all Confidential Information in confidence." Making the return of confidential information optional weakens the obligation to hold all Confidential Information in confidence, creating a clear and direct conflict, which falls under the definition of a structural flaw that causes in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information *regardless of whether* the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.explanation: By adding "regardless of whether" the Receiving Party can demonstrate the exceptions, the original limitations on obligations are now nullified. This contradicts the initial intention of Section 6, which established specific, demonstrable conditions under which confidentiality obligations *do not* apply. Now the obligations don't apply regardless of whether they can demonstrate. This contradicts the intent of Section 4, which states the Receiving Party 'shall hold all Confidential Information in confidence'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Disclosure and Protection of Confidential Information. The Receiving Party shall (i) hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement and (ii) use Confidential Information only for the NDA Purpose and for no other purpose without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds "regardless of whether", negating the need for the Receiving Party to demonstrate any of the listed exceptions to confidentiality. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which states that the Receiving Party 'shall hold all Confidential Information in confidence, and shall not disclose Confidential Information except as expressly permitted under the terms of this Agreement'. The change removes the limitations on confidentiality obligations, creating a direct conflict with the requirement to hold information in confidence unless specific conditions are met.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, *only* during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "only" restricts the scope of the agreement to Confidential Information disclosed *during* the term, contradicting the original clause "whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement." This contradiction creates uncertainty about whether pre-existing confidential information shared before the agreement's effective date is protected. This now contradicts section 5 where it mentions, 'whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement'
perturbation.contradicted_text: Scope and Term of Agreement. This Agreement pertains to all Confidential Information that is disclosed o r otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Affiliates to the Receiving Party in connection with the NDA Purpose, only during the term of this Agreement. The term of this Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date set f orth below and shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds 'only' to the sentence, restricting the agreement to Confidential Information disclosed only during the term of the agreement. This directly contradicts the original text in the contradicted section, which states that the agreement pertains to Confidential Information disclosed 'whether prior to or during the term of this Agreement'. This is a clear and direct conflict, as the changed text narrows the scope of the agreement in a way that is incompatible with the original scope.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date set f orth below and shall continue for a period of twelve (12) months thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement shall commence as of the Effective Date set f orth below and shall continue *until either party provides written notice of termination*.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the fixed term of 12 months with termination upon written notice introduces ambiguity. Originally, the agreement had a definite end date. Now, it can be terminated at any time, contradicting the initial intention of a 12-month commitment. This in turn makes the section on 'Limitation of Obligations' in section 6 more relevant, because a lot of confidential information can be leaked once one of the parties decides to terminate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Limitation on Obligations. The obligations of the Receiving Party with respect to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information shall not apply with respect to any Confidential Information to the extent the Receiving Party can demonstrate that such Confidential Information: (a) is generally known to the public at the time of disclosure or becomes generally known through no breach of this Agreement; (b) is in the Receiving Party’s rightful possession at the time of disclosure without an obligation to hold it in Page 2 of 2 confidence; (c) becomes known to the Receiving Party without obligation of confidence through disclosure by a third party h aving the legal right to disclose such information; (d) is independently developed by the Receiving Party without access or reference to, or reliance upon, the Confidential Information ; or (e) is or becomes disclosed through the ordinary course of the NDA Purpose or as an element of any marketing plan intended to be released in the public domain or to targeted geographic public domain .
perturbation.justification: The original text established a fixed 12-month term. The changed text introduces the possibility of termination upon written notice, removing the fixed term. This directly contradicts the implicit assumption of a 12-month term in the 'Limitation on Obligations' section. If the agreement is terminated early, the limitations on obligations might be insufficient to protect the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information, which creates a conflict in the scope of obligations. Therefore, a clear contradiction exists because the initial fixed term of 12 months doesn't exist in the changed text, creating an in-text contradiction according to the definition of structural flaws and inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: AGX and CANDIDATE desire to exchange information and to engage in discussions concerning AGX’s agent program and Candidate’s qualifications to establish a freight Agency Relationship.
perturbation.changed_text: AGX and CANDIDATE *are required* to exchange information and to engage in discussions concerning AGX’s agent program and Candidate’s qualifications to establish a freight Agency Relationship.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "desire" to "are required" transforms a voluntary intention into a mandatory obligation. This implies that both parties *must* disclose information, even if they're hesitant or have reservations. The fact that both parties are 'required' now, and it wasn't before, contradicts the section on 'Confidential Information' where either party can choose to desingate or not designate confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information. As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” shall mean all information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable by proper means by other persons and is either (i) designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Disclosing Party prior to or at the time of disclosure or (ii) information that a reasonable person should recognize as being confidential or proprietary, based on the nature of the information and/or the circumstances of its disclosure. By way of example, Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information regarding a Party’s products, product development, services, agreements and contracts, business operations, business plans and financial affairs; employees and other personnel; vendors and customers; marketing plans and strategies; processes, compensation formulas, methods and specifications; and information technology network. Additionally, t he fact of the Parties discussions regarding a p otential business relationship shall be deemed Confidential Information subject to the protections of this Agreement. Confidential Information may be either the property of the Disclosing Party or information provided to the Disclosing Party by a third party.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'desire' to 'are required' in the changed text creates a mandatory obligation to exchange information. This directly contradicts the 'Confidential Information' section, which allows parties to designate information as confidential or not. If both parties are required to disclose information, it negates the option to designate certain information as confidential and withhold it. The change directly clashes with the optionality provided in the 'Confidential Information' section.
